coredata.dc:title: Prompt in situ synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots from jaggery for parallel determination of iron, chromium and manganese in environmental samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Kolekar, Akanksha G.
Nille, Omkar S.
Gunjal, Datta B.
Naik, Vaibhav M.
Nguyen Ngoc, Quang
Sohn, Daewon
Kolekar, Govind B.
Gokavi, Gavisiddappa S.
More, Vishalkumar R.
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots (S-CDs) from jaggery using a microwave-assisted method and their application in the detection of iron (Fe), chromium (Cr), and manganese (Mn) is presented in this study. The microwave synthesis method involved the carbonization of jaggery in presence of sulphuric acid solution under controlled conditions. The resulting S-CDs exhibited excellent photoluminescent properties, characterized by strong fluorescence emission in the visible range. Structural and optical characterization of the synthesized S-CDs was performed using transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The S-CDs demonstrated high fluorescence selectivity, excellent liner range and limit of detection towards these metals, enabling the efficient detection of iron, chromium, and manganese in environmental samples. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching of the S-CDs in the presence of Fe, Cr, and Mn ions was used for quantitative analysis, providing a rapid and sensitive detection platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S doped CDs
Fluorescent probe
Metal ion sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of quantum yield of S-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge to the Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur (MS), India, and Department of Chemistry, New College, Kolhapur for providing facilities. As well as Department of Chemistry, Hanyang University, Seoul, South Korea for providing characterization facilities, Daewon Sohn and Ngoc Quang Nguyen thanks to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) 2020R1A6A1A06046728 and 2022R1A2C1010580.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized UV–vis, excitation and emission spectra of S-CDs.
(a) Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) image b) Bar diagram of average particle size and c) SAED pattern of S-CDs.
a) XRD spectrum, b) EDAX analysis of S-CDs.
a) Survey XPS spectrum, Deconvoluted spectra of b) C 1 s, c) O 1 s and d) High resolution S 2p of S-CDs.
Stability of S-CDs in different pH solutions.
A) effect of irradiation time, b) effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence of s-cds.

Fluorescence quenching spectra of S-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (a), Cr6+ (c), and Mn7+ (e) ions and (b) Linearity curve of fluorescence quenching for Fe3+ (b), Cr6+ (d), and Mn7+(f).
Fluorescence decay spectrum for the S-CDs in presences of a) Fe3+, b) Cr6+, and c) Mn7+ ions.

a) Effect of different interfering cations, and anions on selective determination of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions in water samples, b) F/F0 bar graph showing the selectivity of S-CDs towards different metal ions.
Analysis result of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions in water samples from river water by the standard addition method (n = 3).

entry.bold: studied amount of standard metal ions added (μg/mL)

head.ce:title: Prompt in situ synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots from jaggery for parallel determination of iron, chromium and manganese in environmental samples

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, The New College, Kolhapur, MS, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots (S-CDs) from jaggery using a microwave-assisted method and their application in the detection of iron (Fe), chromium (Cr), and manganese (Mn) is presented in this study. The microwave synthesis method involved the carbonization of jaggery in presence of sulphuric acid solution under controlled conditions. The resulting S-CDs exhibited excellent photoluminescent properties, characterized by strong fluorescence emission in the visible range. Structural and optical characterization of the synthesized S-CDs was performed using transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The S-CDs demonstrated high fluorescence selectivity, excellent liner range and limit of detection towards these metals, enabling the efficient detection of iron, chromium, and manganese in environmental samples. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching of the S-CDs in the presence of Fe, Cr, and Mn ions was used for quantitative analysis, providing a rapid and sensitive detection platform.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of heavy metal ions in water resources poses a threat to the habitats of living organisms and has the potential to accumulate in various parts of the body. These ions can adversely affect people's health and disrupt the food chain. Among the various nanoscale toxic heavy metal ions, Iron (III), Chromium (VI), and Manganese (VII) pose a serious threat to human well-being due to their widespread presence in aqueous form when present above permissible level [1,2]. Hence, the detection and monitoring the level of heavy metal ions is become crucial. There are multiple analytical techniques available for the identification and measurement of metal ions in water samples. These methods differ in terms of their sensitivity, and instrumental needs. The common conventional analysis methods such as inductively coupled plasma/atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XRF), and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), Electrochemical [3–12] have been utilized for detecting heavy metal ions over time. Although, these techniques offer high selectivity and sensitivity, they have drawbacks such as expensive equipment, lack of portability, lengthy analysis time, and complex sample preparation steps.

Fluorescence methods present several advantages over other analytical techniques for the detection and quantification of analytes, including metal ions, in various sample types. Here are some key benefits of fluorescence methods: high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, capability for multiplexing, real-time monitoring, non-destructive and non-invasive analysis, wide dynamic range, and ease of operation. These advantages render fluorescence methods highly valuable for the detection and analysis of metal ions, particularly in areas such as environmental monitoring, biomedical research, and chemical analysis [13].

In this context, several nanotechnology-based detection systems have recently been proposed [14]. Various fluorescent nanomaterials, including carbon-based and metallic nanoparticles, offer potential solutions to address these environmental challenges [15,16]. Among them, carbon dots synthesized through simple methods have gained attention due to their sustainability, affordability, biocompatibility, and user-friendliness.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of carbon-based fluorescent nanoparticles primarily composed of carbon atoms, possessing distinctive optical, electronic, and chemical characteristics. As a result, they have garnered significant interest across various scientific and technological domains. CDs are generally small in size, ranging from a few nanometers to a few tens of nanometers, and exhibit exceptional water solubility and photostability. CDs represent a captivating and adaptable class of nanomaterials with a broad array of applications. Their fluorescence can be tuned, they are biocompatible, and their surface properties are unique, making them appealing in fields such as biology, optoelectronics, sensing, and environmental remediation. Continued research and advancements in carbon dot synthesis, surface engineering, and the exploration of their fundamental properties unlocked many possibilities for these remarkable nanoparticles. Moreover, carbon dots offer a versatile platform for metal ion detection. Their distinct optical properties, ease of functionalization, and compatibility with aqueous environments make them highly suitable for diverse applications, ranging from environmental monitoring to biomedical diagnostics [13].

In this study, we have presented a one-step microwave method for synthesizing S-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) using easily accessible precursors, namely jaggery and sulphuric acid. This method is simple and efficient which offers great advantages over the other methods of production of S-CDs. Our findings demonstrate that the fluorescent properties of S-CDs exhibit sensitivity towards Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+. Notably, S-CDs exhibit remarkable sensitivity and selectivity as well. When employed as a sensor for detecting heavy metals in water samples, achieved satisfactory recovery ratios. It is noteworthy that the utilization of jaggery, a commonly available traditional sweetener derived from sugarcane juice, directly in carbon dots synthesis eliminates the need for complex starting materials, simplifying the synthesis process. To the best of our knowledge, the existence of a sensor capable of simultaneously detecting Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ is rare, and the research on using single fluorophore carbon dots for detecting these metals in water samples is also limited. Thus, present work contributes cost effective tool for environmental monitoring.

The jaggery was purchased from a local market. All the reagents required for the experiment were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The used all metal salts such as BaCl2, CuSO4·5H2O, NiCl2, K2Cr2O7, KMnO4, ZnSO4·7H2O, Fe(NO3)3, CaCl2·2H2O, PbCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, HgCl2, NaCl, SnCl2, FeCl2, CuSO4, LiCl, Na2S, NaNO3, Na2SO4, etc. were purchased from S d fine-chem. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). The stock solutions of every compound were prepared by dissolving their corresponding compounds in doubly distilled water and used throughout the experiment.

A measured amount of jaggery was placed in a beaker, and a known volume of concentrated sulphuric acid was added to it. The acid was used to assist in the hydrolysis of jaggery and facilitate the formation of carbon dots. Subsequently, the jaggery solution was transferred to a microwave-safe container, which was carefully positioned in a microwave oven. The solution was subjected to a power of 720 MW for a precise duration of 300 s. This controlled microwave heating resulted in the transformation of the solution's color from colorless to brownish, indicative of carbon dot formation. After the completion of reaction, the solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature and subsequently subjected to centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 15 min. This centrifugation step served to separate and eliminate larger or agglomerated carbon particles, with the supernatant being collected. The collected supernatant was then neutralized using Na2CO3 and further filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to eliminate any remaining larger particles. Lastly, to obtain a pure solution of carbon dots, the filtrate underwent dialysis against double distilled water for a duration of 24 h, using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3500 D. The resulting solution of pure carbon dots was then stored at freezing temperature to preserve its integrity for subsequent experimentation.

The FP-8300 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jasco, Japan) was used to measure the fluorescence spectra with a 1 cm path length and 5 nm slit width for both the excitation and emission filters. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were acquired using a Specord Analytica Jena spectrophotometer. The particle size and morphology were determined by examining them under a transmission electron microscope (PHILIPS, CM-200 model) with an accelerating voltage range of 20–200 kV. The functional groups of the S-CDs were analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Jasco 680-plus Tokyo, Japan). The crystallinity of the sample was examined through X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Bruker X-ray diffractometer (Germany) operating at a voltage of 45 kV and a current of 40 mA. The chemical composition of the sample was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with an X-ray source Al Kα excitation (Termo Scientific) at energy of 1486.6 eV. The fluorescence lifetime was measured using a Time Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) spectrophotometer (HORIBA JobinYvon IBH, nano LED-07) at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of quantum yield of S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantitative photoluminescence capability of carbon dots is assessed through the measurement of quantum yield. This calculation involves a comparison with a standard compound of having known quantum yield. For this experiment, an aqueous solution of quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (with a refractive index η of 1.33 and a QY of 0.54) was selected as the standard compound. Both compounds' absorbance (maintained below 0.10) and fluorescence intensity were measured. These values were then utilized to determine the quantum yield through the following method:
(1)
Q Y =
QY
R
x
I
I R
x
A R
A
x
η
2
η
R
2
Where, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, A denotes the absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and the subscript R pertains to the standard compound.

The detection of metal ion such as Fe3+ was conducted at room temperature. In a typical assay, 0.5 mL of S-CDs dispersion was mixed with various concentrations of standard Fe3+ ions in separate test tubes with maintaing appropriate pH. Subsequently, the entire mixture was diluted to 10.0 mL using double distilled water. After allowing the solution to react for 5 min at room temperature, fluorescence spectra were recorded at 360 nm excitation. The same procedure was followed for detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions also.

Furthermore, to assess the selectivity of the proposed fluorescent probe, stock solutions of different cations were added in the solution of S-CDs and diluted to the fixed volume by maintaining pH. Eventually, the fluorescence of each solution were recorded by keeping 360 nm as an excitation wavelength and the slit width of excitation and emission monochromators maintained at 5 nm throughout the experiment.

The water sample was taken from a nearby river and then followed nominal pre-treatment, involving boiling and filtration, to eliminate suspended organic and clay particles. Next, three different concentrations of each standard solutions containing Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ were added to the treated water sample. These prepared samples were then introduced into the sensing system, allowed to equilibrate for 5 min, and their fluorescence measurements were recorded.

The characterization of carbon dots involves the analysis of their fluorescence properties, which are crucial for understanding their optical behavior and potential applications. The as synthesized S-CDs were highly water soluble and characterized by different optical and surface analysis techniques.

The optical properties such as absorption and fluorescence are highly characteristic of carbon dots. Hence, these optical properties of as synthesized S-CDs were confirmed by recording UV–visible and fluorescence spectra. The UV–Visible absorption spectrum shows peak at 267 nm which is attributed to the π −π∗ (C=C) and the slight hump is due to the n–π ∗ transitions of the carbonyl (C=O) groups of S-CDs (Fig. 1 ). The inset showing photographic image of S-CDs under day light and UV light. Additionally, the fluorescence spectrum of S-CDs depicted in the Fig. 1 shows the emission wavelength is at 442 nm when excited at 360 nm. The intensity of emission is high indicating that synthesized S-CDs possess a pronounced fluorescence property. It is expressed in terms of quantum yield. The quantum yield of synthesized S-CDs was found to be 8.24 %. It is highly consisted with reported sulphur doped carbon dots.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) provides valuable information of carbon dots about their size, shape, structure, distribution, surface morphology, and aggregation state. These insights are crucial for understanding the properties and performance of carbon dots and guiding their application in various fields.

The Fig. 2 a shows spherical morphology of the carbon dots which exhibits a range of sizes, with diameters typically in the 8–13 nm range (Fig. 2b). Some dots are uniform in size, while others show slight variations in size and shape. The dots appear to be well-dispersed and evenly distributed throughout the image, suggesting a homogeneous population. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 2 c) do not shows any bright rings or vague rings which indicates that S-CDs have amorphous nature rather than crystalline.

The XRD pattern of S-CDs displays broad peak at 2θ ≈ 23.4° which is attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms as depicted in Fig. 3 (a). The absence of sharp peaks indicates the lack of long-range ordering in the carbon dots. Such broad peak is assigned to the carbon dots, often have an amorphous or nanocrystalline structure, which results in a broad, featureless XRD pattern without well-defined peaks [17,18].

The amorphous nature of S-CDs revealed by XRD analysis is consistent with SAED pattern. Additionally, an intense peak at 2θ ≈ 26.5° is observed in the spectrum which is attributed to the formation of a C-S composite [17].

The presence of sulfur atoms in carbon dots was confirmed through X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Subsequently, the elemental analysis of the carbon dots was characterized using Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDX) Fig. 3 (b) prominently showed three main peaks corresponding to the carbon, oxygen, and sulphur element. The presences of other peaks are related impurities. The results indicated that the composition of CDs mainly consists of carbon, oxygen, and sulfur, which reveals successful doping of sulphur atom in carbon dots. The relative intensity of these peaks can indicate the presence and abundance of these elements within the carbon dots.

Moreover, the elemental analysis of S-CDs was further ascertained by XPS analysis. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 a) of S-CDs shows three strong elemental peaks of C1s, and O1s and S2p at 284.98, 532.96 and 169.20 eV, respectively reveals presence of carbon, oxygen and sulphur in the synthesized material. The XPS spectrum primarily showed carbon-related peaks due to the carbon-based nature of the dots. The synthesized carbon dots have oxygen-containing surface functional groups like hydroxyl (–OH) or carbonyl (−C=O) hence additional peaks related to oxygen is observed which usually appear at higher binding energies than the C1s peak. The presence of sulphur peak indicates doping of sulpher in carbon dots [19].

A deconvoluted XPS spectrum of the C1s peak (Fig. 4b) of carbon dots provides valuable insights into the chemical composition and bonding environment of carbon atoms within the material. In this spectrum, the prominent peak appears at approximately 284.9 eV representing carbon atoms in sp2 hybridized carbon–carbon double bonds (C=C) commonly found in the graphitic structure of carbon dots. This component signifies the presence of aromatic and graphitic domains within the carbon dots. A distinct peak around 285.6 eV, is assigned to the C=N groups indicating the presence of carbon atoms bonded to nitrogen atoms also. A discernible peak appears at approximately 288.3 eV, corresponding to carbon atoms engaged in carbonyl (C = O) functional groups.

The Fig. 4 c depicts high resolution XPS Spectrum of O1s which shows a peak appears at approximately 531.8 eV, representing oxygen atoms bonded to carbon atoms in hydroxyl (–OH) functional groups. A second peak also emerged around 533.1 eV, corresponding to oxygen atoms involved carbonyl functional groups. These groups are indicative of oxygen bridges between adjacent carbon atoms in the carbon dots' structure, contributing to the material's stability and surface chemistry.

The most promising deconvoluted XPS spectrum of the S2p peak (Fig. 4d) of carbon dots provides insights into the sulfur-containing functional groups and chemical environments within the material. In the deconvoluted XPS Spectrum of S2p, a peak appears at approximately 170.4 eV, representing sulfur atoms involved in sulfide (S-O) or carbon–sulfur (C-S) bonds (C-SO2). A distinct peak near at 171.6 eV, corresponding to sulfur atoms in C2SO is observed.

This various sulphur peaks indicates the successful in situ doping of sulphur atom into the framework carbon dots. This peak also reveals the presence of surface hydroxyl, carbonyl groups, which can contribute to the material's surface reactivity, hydrophilicity, and potential interactions with other molecules or surfaces.

FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 5 ) was utilized to identify the surface functionality of S-CDs. The absorption band observed at 3306 cm−1 corresponds to O-H stretching vibrations, while the absorption band at 1626 cm−1 indicates the presence of a sp2 hybridized carbon and is attributed to the C = C stretching vibration. The presence of such peaks suggests the existence of aromatic structures or small graphitic domains within the carbon dots. Additionally, the stretching frequency at 1220 cm−1 is associated with the C-O groups, and the absorption at 1370 cm−1 is attributed to –CH3 deformation/C-H bending. The FT-IR absorption spectrum reveals the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of the S-CDs. These hydrophilic groups significantly contribute to the exceptional water solubility of the synthesized S-CDs [20]. The XPS and IR results of S-CDs are consistent with each other.

The stability of synthesized S-CDs has great importance while using it as a chemical sensor in solution state. Therefore, the stability in terms of different pH (2–12) conditions has been assessed. The pH of the solution was maintained by using the appropriate mixture of HCl: NaOH. Fig. 6 displays effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of S-CDs. It indicates that pH of the solution have marginal effect on intensity. S-CDs are stable in wide range of pH, therefore synthesized S-CDs can be used as a probe for the detection of analyte in highly acidic, neutral and extreme basic condition as well [21]. In this method, for the detection of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions pH = 7 was maintained throughout the experiment.

The photostability of S-CDs was also evaluated by varying the irradiation time. The sample was continuously irradiated upto 60 min with 10 min time interval under a Xenon lamp at excitation wavelength at 360 nm. The Fig. 7 a indicates effect of irradiation on emission intensity of S-CDs. This figure shows that the fluorescence of S-CDs almost remain constant throughout the irradiation time. The results suggest that synthesized S-CDs are highly photostable therefore it can used as an excellent fluorescent probe for chemical analysis.

In addition, luminescence stability of S-CDs in highly ionic solution was evaluated. The Fig. 7b indicate that the emission intensity response of S-CDs remained consistent across the NaCl concentration range of 0.1–1.0 M. This independence from salt concentration in fluorescence further indicates the high stability of S-CDs even in extremely ionic solutions, suggesting their potential utilization as probes in such environments.

The quenching of fluorescence of S-CDs was analyzed with different concentrations of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions under the optimized experimental conditions. Fig. 8 a, c & e demonstrate the gradual reduction in intensity of fluorescence of S-CDs with the increase in concentrations of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+. It is noteworthy that nearly 96 % of the emission signal of S-CDs was quenched by 100.0 μg/mL of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions. These results indicate the sensitivity of the proposed system (S-CDs) towards these analyte concentrations.

Therefore, we hypothesized that the quantification of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ could be easily accomplished using the developed probe. However, to establish a method for determining the concentrations of these analytes, a linear correlation between analyte concentration and probe intensity is essential. The standard Stern-Volmer quenching relationship is used for each case to evaluate the analytical linear range of detection. The Stern-Volmer equation is represented by eq (2).
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
Q

Where, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of metal ions respectively. The Stern-Volmer constant is denoted by Ksv, and [Q] signifies the concentration of metal ions. Fig. 8 b, d & f is the plot of F0/F versus concentration of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ which demonstrates a linear relationship between fluorescence response and the concentration of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions was within the range of 0.0–0.90, 0.0–0.50 and 0.0–0.90 μg/mL with the correlation coefficient (R2) 0.997, 0.987 and 0.99 respectively.

Subsequently, the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions were also evaluated by using following equation.
(3)
L i m i t
o f
D e t e c t i o n
L O D
=
3.3 σ
K

Where, σ is standard deviation and K is the slope of the calibration curve. The Stern-Volmer plot allowed us to determine the limit of detection (LOD) for the developed sensing system, which was found to be 0.38 μg/mL for Fe3+, 0.21 μg/mL for Cr6+, and 0.31 μg/mL for Mn7+ ions, respectively. The mentioned linearity, correlation coefficient, and LOD collectively demonstrate excellent precision of the developed method, making it as a promising and reliable for assaying Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions in water samples. The developed S-CDs based sensor was compared with previously published research articles in terms of linearity & LOD for metal ion detection. It reveals that developed sensor is in good aggrement with other carbon dot based sensor system (Table S1).

It is essential to understand the quenching mechanism of fluorescence of S-CDs in presence Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions, since it provides valuable insights into the interaction between carbon dots and ions, helping to understand the underlying chemical and physical processes involved in the quenching phenomenon

The fluorescence is quenched through different kinds of quenching mechanism and is depends on the properties of the metal ions and the surface functionalization of the carbon dots. To ascertain the quenching mechanism, the life-time measurement (TCSPC) experiment was performed at the excitation wavelength (360 nm) of S-CDs for a 440 nm emission wavelength, a single exponential decay curve was obtained. The lifetime of S-CDs in the presence and absence of different concentrations of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions is shown in Fig. 9 . The lifetime of S-CDs in the absences Fe3+ was 4.42 ns and in presence of different concentration of Fe3+ were 4.37 and 4.30 ns which reveal no explicit change in lifetime (Fig. 9a). Therefore, this type of quenching is a static type of fluorescence quenching of S-CDs due to the ground state complex formation between S-CDs and Fe3+ ions [22]. Furthermore, Fig. 9 b indicates, the lifetime of S-CDs in presence and absences of Cr6+ ions, remained almost stagnant viz. 4.20, 3.80 and 3.20 ns. It suggests that the again static type of quenching of fluorescence of S-CDs. Nevertheless, the inner filter effect might be the prime cause of such quenching since absorption spectrum of Cr6+ ions and excitation spectrum of S-CDs overlaps appreciably [23,24]. Hence, fluorescence of S-CDs was quenched with increasing concentration of Cr6+ ions. In addition, the UV–Vis. absorbance spectra of S-CDs in presence and absence of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions metal was studied (Fig. S1). The results indicate that, the S-CDs interacts with the Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ metal ions and diminishes their characteristic absorption peaks. The diminishing of peaks of pristine metal ions and development of new peaks after interaction with S-CDs results is due to the complex formation. The inset image showing observable colour change of metal ions after interaction with S-CDs again reveals the formation of complex.

On the other hand, the lifetime of S-CDs in the presence of Mn7+ was (4.25 ns) altered or decreased (0.09 & 0.08 ns) with an increasing concentration of Mn7+ (Fig. 9c). These findings reveal a dynamic type of fluorescence quenching of S-CDs due to the excited state interaction between S-CDs and Mn7+ ions [25–28].

The selectivity of S-CDs pertains to their capacity to interact or respond exclusively to specific analytes or target substances while disregarding other interfering species. This desirable characteristic allows carbon dots to find utility in diverse applications, including sensing, imaging, and drug delivery. Considering the analytical application point, the selectivity of the proposed method was thoroughly investigated under the same experimental conditions.

The developed probe was subjected to examination with various environmentally relevant cations, such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Na+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Cr6+, Cu2+, Li+, Mn7+, anions such as S2-, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2- sulphates etc.

As depicted in Fig. 10 , a noticeable change in fluorescence intensity was observed upon the addition of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions due to their ability to interact with S-CDs in different manner. This interaction ultimately resulted in quenching of fluorescence of S-CDs. On the other hand, other ions, despite being used in higher concentrations than the analyte ions, did not exhibit a significant effect on the fluorescence intensity. Whereas, the Fig. 10 b depicts the F/F0 bar graphs of S-CDs towards selectivity of different metal ions. The obtained results showing the diminished bar graphs for Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions. In addition, to investigate the selectivity of S-CDs towards different salts of Fe3+ ions with counter anions like nitrate, sulphate and chloride were studied (Fig. S2). The results indicates that the S-CDs showing nearly similar quenching for Fe3+ ions with varying salts. This proves that the S-CDs selectively quenches the Fe3+ ions and does not interfere with counter anion also.

These results demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching of the S-CDs is selective to Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions, distinguishing them from common ions present in water. The sensitivity, rapid response to quenching, and selectivity of the developed probe indicate its potential as an efficient fluorescent sensor for monitoring Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ in real water samples.

The practicality of the developed method for detecting Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ in real water samples was evaluated by using a river water sample spiked with different concentrations of standard Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ solutions. As anticipated, the emission intensity decreased as the amount of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions increased. The percent recoveries and relative standard deviation were calculated using the calibration curve and are listed in Table 1 . The results showed that all recoveries fell within the range of 96–101 %, and the relative standard deviation ranged from 1.14 % to 3.44 %. These findings indicate that the method is highly accurate and reliable for determining Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ in real water samples.

In summary, we have developed a straightforward microwave in situ method to synthesize sulphur doped carbon dots (S-CDs) from jaggery, which proved to be a rapid approach compared to existing methods. The synthesized S-CDs exhibited remarkable stability in different pH solutions. It further exhibited fluorescence quenching when exposed to Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ ions due to their complexation, inner filter and electron transfer ability with S-CDs respectively. This developed sensor offers several advantages, including sensitivity and the capability to simultaneously detect multiple (three) ions by using a single probe. Additionally, it possesses a lower detection limit, fast response time, making it a promising platform for the accurate and reliable detection of Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn7+ in real samples.

Akanksha G. Kolekar: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Omkar S. Nille: Formal analysis, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Datta B. Gunjal: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Vaibhav M. Naik: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Quang Nguyen Ngoc: Data curation, Formal analysis. Daewon Sohn: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Govind B. Kolekar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision. Gavisiddappa S. Gokavi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Vishalkumar R. More: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge to the Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur (MS), India, and Department of Chemistry, New College, Kolhapur for providing facilities. As well as Department of Chemistry, Hanyang University, Seoul, South Korea for providing characterization facilities, Daewon Sohn and Ngoc Quang Nguyen thanks to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) 2020R1A6A1A06046728 and 2022R1A2C1010580.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115672.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
